description: CUDA's multi-level memory system: global memory coalescing, shared memory bank conflicts, L1/L2 caching, register pressure, constant/texture memory, and unified memory. Memory optimization is the single most impactful class of CUDA optimizations.
sources: books/Programming Massively Parallel Processors.pdf (Kirk & Hwu) — Ch5 CUDA Memories, Ch6 Performance Considerations
books/cuda_by_example.pdf (Sanders & Kandrot) — Ch5 Thread Cooperation, Ch6 Constant Memory and Events, Ch7 Texture Memory

concepts.title: Global Memory
concepts.preciseDefinition: The primary large-capacity off-chip DRAM on the GPU (HBM2e/HBM3 on data-center GPUs). Accessible by all threads in all blocks. H100 SXM: 80 GB, 3.35 TB/s bandwidth. Access latency is 400–800 clock cycles but can be hidden by warp switching. Global memory is organized in 128-byte cache line segments; optimal access requires all threads in a warp to access consecutive addresses (coalescing).
keyFacts: H100 HBM3: 80 GB capacity, 3.35 TB/s bandwidth; A100: 80 GB, 2 TB/s; B200: 192 GB HBM3e, 8 TB/s
Access latency: ~400 cycles for L2 miss; ~200 cycles for L2 hit
Cache line size: 128 bytes = 32 FP32 values = one warp's worth of contiguous floats
ECC (error-correcting code) reduces effective bandwidth by ~6% when enabled
cudaMalloc returns device memory pointers; cudaMemcpy transfers between host and device
commonMisconceptions: Global memory is shared with the CPU — it requires explicit cudaMemcpy or Unified Memory
All global memory accesses hit at L2 — L2 is 50 MB on H100; working sets larger than L2 thrash
cudaMalloc is equivalent to malloc in speed — cudaMalloc involves driver calls; prefer pre-allocation

concepts.title: Memory Coalescing
concepts.preciseDefinition: When all 32 threads of a warp access memory within the same 128-byte cache line (contiguous, aligned addresses), the hardware services the entire warp in a single memory transaction. Non-coalesced access (e.g., column-major access of a row-major matrix) requires up to 32 separate transactions — a 32× bandwidth degradation.
concepts.source: gpu-glossary/perf/memory-coalescing.md; cuda-mode-lectures/lecture_008/coalesce.cu; books/Programming Massively Parallel Processors Ch6 §6.2 Global Memory Bandwidth
keyFacts: Ideal: 32 threads × 4B = 128B = one cache line = one transaction
Worst case: 32 threads hitting 32 different cache lines = 32 transactions = 32× slowdown
Coalescing requires: consecutive thread indices accessing consecutive addresses
Row-major array[i][j] accessed with i=blockIdx.x*blockDim.x+threadIdx.x is coalesced (row access)
Column-major access (stride = width) causes non-coalescing; use matrix transpose as intermediate step
Nsight Compute shows 'Global Load Efficiency' metric (ideal: 100%)
commonMisconceptions: Any sequential memory access is coalesced — the warp must access within a single cache line
Coalescing only matters for reads — write coalescing is equally critical
Padding arrays never helps coalescing — padding to align rows to 128B enables coalesced column access

exercises.title: Matrix Transpose
exercises.whatItTeaches: Why naïve column-write transpose achieves only ~1/32 of peak bandwidth; how to use shared memory as a staging buffer for coalesced writes; padding shared memory to avoid bank conflicts.
exercises.starterCodeHint: __shared__ float tile[TILE][TILE+1]; tile[threadIdx.y][threadIdx.x] = in[row*N+col]; __syncthreads(); out[transposed_pos] = tile[threadIdx.x][threadIdx.y];
exercises.testCaseHint: Benchmark naïve vs tiled vs tiled+padded at 1024×1024 and 4096×4096; verify out[j][i]==in[i][j]

concepts.title: Shared Memory
concepts.preciseDefinition: A software-managed SRAM scratchpad local to an SM, shared by all threads in a block. Latency ~4 cycles (vs ~400 for global memory). Organized into 32 banks of 4 bytes each; consecutive 32-bit words map to consecutive banks. Capacity is configurable: on Ampere/Hopper the L1 + shared pool is 228 KB per SM, with shared memory configurable up to 228 KB (minus L1).
concepts.source: gpu-glossary/device-software/shared-memory.md; docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-c-best-practices-guide/#shared-memory; books/Programming Massively Parallel Processors Ch5 §5.2 CUDA Device Memory Types; books/cuda_by_example.pdf Ch5 §5.3 Shared Memory and Synchronization
keyFacts: Declared with __shared__ qualifier; allocated per-block; lifetime is the block's lifetime
32 banks, each 4 bytes wide; bank index = (address / 4) % 32
Broadcast exception: if all threads read the same address, it is served in one transaction
Dynamic allocation: extern __shared__ float smem[]; size passed as 3rd kernel launch parameter
Ampere: up to 164 KB per block with cudaFuncSetAttribute(f, cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize, size)
commonMisconceptions: Shared memory is automatically L1 cache — they are logically separate; L1 caches global memory, shared memory is explicit
Bank conflict happens when two threads read the same bank — conflict requires different addresses in same bank
Shared memory is global to all blocks — it is private to each block; different blocks have independent copies

concepts.title: Shared Memory Bank Conflict
concepts.preciseDefinition: A performance serialization that occurs when two or more threads in a warp access different addresses that map to the same shared memory bank. The accesses are serialized, reducing throughput by a factor equal to the maximum number of conflicting accesses (the 'conflict degree'). A 2-way conflict halves throughput; a 32-way conflict (all threads hitting one bank) reduces to 1/32 throughput.
keyFacts: 32 banks; bank(addr) = (addr >> 2) & 31
Addresses 128 bytes apart map to the same bank (32 banks × 4 bytes = 128B period)
Column access of a 32-wide row-major shared array: all 32 threads hit bank 0 — 32-way conflict
Padding fix: declare tile[32][33] instead of tile[32][32]; moves columns to different banks
Nsight Compute: 'Shared Memory Bank Conflicts' counter; ideal = 0
commonMisconceptions: Bank conflicts only occur with column accesses — any access pattern with stride=32 causes conflicts
Padding wastes significant memory — 1 extra element per row (4B per row) is negligible overhead
Broadcast (all threads same address) causes a bank conflict — broadcast is conflict-free in hardware

exercises.title: Tiled Matrix Multiplication
exercises.whatItTeaches: How tiling GEMM into TILE×TILE blocks reduces global memory traffic by a factor of TILE (CGMA ratio improves from 1 to TILE); shared memory lifecycle (load → syncthreads → compute); double-buffering technique to overlap loads with computation.
exercises.starterCodeHint: __shared__ float As[TILE][TILE], Bs[TILE][TILE]; for (int t=0; t<K/TILE; t++) { As[ty][tx]=A[row*K+t*TILE+tx]; Bs[ty][tx]=B[(t*TILE+ty)*N+col]; __syncthreads(); for(int k=0;k<TILE;k++) sum+=As[ty][k]*Bs[k][tx]; __syncthreads(); }
exercises.testCaseHint: Compare against CPU reference at M=N=K=512; benchmark vs cuBLAS at M=N=K=1024/2048/4096; verify CGMA = TILE for shared memory version vs CGMA=1 for naive
exercises.source: cuda-samples/0_Introduction/matrixMul/; llm.c/dev/cuda/matmul_forward.cu; books/Programming Massively Parallel Processors Ch5 §5.3 A Strategy for Reducing Global Memory Traffic; Ch5 Exercise 5.2

exercises.title: Constant Memory Ray Tracer (CUDA by Example)
exercises.whatItTeaches: When and how to use constant memory (__constant__) for read-only broadcast data; why constant memory is faster than global memory when all threads in a warp read the same address (broadcast cache); cudaMemcpyToSymbol usage; measuring speedup vs global memory version.
exercises.starterCodeHint: __constant__ Sphere dev_spheres[SPHERE_COUNT]; // cudaMemcpyToSymbol(dev_spheres, spheres, sizeof(Sphere)*SPHERE_COUNT); // In kernel: access dev_spheres[i] — broadcast if all threads read same i
exercises.testCaseHint: Render 20 spheres, 1024×1024 pixels; measure frame time with constant vs global memory; verify identical pixel output
exercises.source: books/cuda_by_example.pdf Ch6 §6.1 Constant Memory Ray Tracing with Constant Memory

concepts.title: L1 and L2 Cache
concepts.preciseDefinition: L1 cache is per-SM (~128 KB on Hopper, shared with shared memory pool), caches global memory accesses transparently. L2 cache is on-chip but shared across all SMs (50 MB on H100). L2 hit latency ~200 cycles; L1 hit ~30 cycles; global ~400 cycles. Hopper introduced L2 residency controls (cudaStreamAttrValue) to pin frequently-accessed data.
keyFacts: H100 L2: 50 MB; A100: 40 MB; V100: 6 MB — L2 growth enables caching larger working sets
L1 caches loads by default; stores may bypass L1 (.cs cache streaming hint)
L1 line size: 128 bytes; L2 line size: 128 bytes
Hopper L2 residency: cudaStreamGetCaptureInfo to set access policy window
Ampere/Hopper unified shared memory/L1/texture cache: programmer controls split
commonMisconceptions: Making data fit in L2 always speeds up the kernel — only helps if the bottleneck is global memory latency
L1 cache is always enabled — L1 must be enabled via compiler flag or cudaFuncSetCacheConfig
L2 cache is per-SM — it is shared across all SMs in the GPU

concepts.title: Register Pressure
concepts.preciseDefinition: The constraint imposed by the SM's finite register file on how many warps (and therefore blocks) can be simultaneously resident. Each SM has 65536 32-bit registers. If a kernel uses 64 registers/thread, then (65536 / 64) / 32 = 32 warps = 1 block of 1024 threads max per SM. Excessive register use reduces occupancy; register spilling to local memory (stack) incurs ~400-cycle latency per access.
keyFacts: SM register file: 65536 × 32-bit registers on Ampere/Hopper (256 KB per SM)
Registers are per-thread; kernel compilation determines register count per thread
--maxrregcount=N nvcc flag limits registers, forcing spilling if N is too low
nvcc -Xptxas=-v reports register usage per kernel
Register spilling: overflow variables stored in L1-cached local memory (slow: ~30-400 cycle latency)
commonMisconceptions: Fewer registers always means better performance — too few triggers spilling which hurts more
Register spilling uses shared memory — local memory spills go to L1 cache, not shared memory
Registers are zeroed on kernel launch — register values are undefined unless initialized by the kernel

concepts.title: Unified Memory
concepts.preciseDefinition: A CUDA feature (cc 6.0+, Pascal+) where a single pointer is valid on both CPU and GPU. The CUDA runtime automatically migrates pages between host and device on demand. Under Linux with Pascal+, demand paging uses GPU page faults to trigger migration. cudaMallocManaged() allocates unified memory; cudaMemPrefetchAsync() pre-migrates pages to avoid fault overhead.
keyFacts: cudaMallocManaged() replaces separate cudaMalloc + cudaMemcpy workflow
Page fault on GPU: ~10,000 cycle penalty per page (4KB) first access — use prefetch to avoid
cudaMemAdvise() hints: cudaMemAdviseSetPreferredLocation, cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy
NVLink systems: pages can be directly accessed by GPU at lower latency via 0-copy
Profiler shows 'Migration Engine' and 'UVM' metrics for unified memory traffic
commonMisconceptions: Unified memory is always slower than explicit transfers — with prefetching it can match manual transfer performance
cudaMallocManaged memory starts on the GPU — it starts on CPU until first GPU access or prefetch
Unified memory eliminates all data movement — it still moves pages, just automatically

exercises.title: Unified Memory with Prefetching
exercises.whatItTeaches: cudaMallocManaged semantics; how page faults impact performance; cudaMemPrefetchAsync to eliminate fault overhead; comparing prefetched vs non-prefetched throughput.
exercises.starterCodeHint: float *a; cudaMallocManaged(&a, N*sizeof(float)); cudaMemPrefetchAsync(a, N*sizeof(float), device_id);
exercises.testCaseHint: Measure kernel time with and without prefetch at N=100M; fault count visible in Nsight Systems

concepts.title: Compute to Global Memory Access (CGMA) Ratio
concepts.preciseDefinition: The number of floating-point operations performed per global memory access in a CUDA kernel (PMPP Ch5 §5.1). CGMA directly controls whether a kernel is memory-bound or compute-bound. A naïve matrix multiply has CGMA=1 (1 FP multiply + 1 FP add per 2 float reads). Tiled matrix multiply with TILE_WIDTH=16 achieves CGMA=16 by reusing each loaded element across 16 computations via shared memory. The GPU's peak CGMA for compute-bound operation (the 'ridge point') is ~295 FLOPs/byte on H100.
concepts.source: books/Programming Massively Parallel Processors Ch5 §5.1 Importance of Memory Access Efficiency
keyFacts: CGMA=1 (naïve matmul): each thread reads 2 floats and does 2 FLOPs — deeply memory-bound on any GPU
CGMA=TILE_WIDTH (tiled matmul): each loaded element reused TILE_WIDTH times — CGMA=32 achieves compute-bound on H100
H100 ridge point: ~989 TFLOPS / 3.35 TB/s ≈ 295 FLOPs/byte — CGMA must exceed this to be compute-bound
CGMA analysis: count FLOPs and bytes in the innermost loop to predict whether kernel is memory- or compute-bound
Increasing CGMA is the primary goal of all memory optimization techniques (tiling, caching, reuse)
commonMisconceptions: Higher CGMA always means better performance — CGMA above the ridge point just means more idle memory bandwidth
CGMA is only for matrix multiply — any kernel with data reuse (convolution, reduction) can analyze CGMA
CGMA is fixed by the algorithm — it depends on implementation: tiling, caching, and register reuse all improve CGMA

concepts.title: Memory Bandwidth
concepts.preciseDefinition: The rate at which data can be transferred between the GPU's compute units and DRAM, measured in bytes/second. Peak memory bandwidth is set by the HBM specification. Achieved bandwidth = (bytes read + bytes written) / kernel_time. The roofline model plots achieved bandwidth against peak to identify memory-bound kernels. A memory-bound kernel's execution time is dominated by DRAM access time, not arithmetic.
concepts.source: gpu-glossary/perf/memory-bandwidth.md; gpu-glossary/perf/memory-bound.md; books/Programming Massively Parallel Processors Ch6 §6.2
keyFacts: H100 SXM HBM3 peak: 3.35 TB/s; A100 HBM2e: 2.0 TB/s; B200 HBM3e: 8.0 TB/s
Ridge point = arithmetic bandwidth / memory bandwidth (FLOPs/byte); above ridge = compute-bound, below = memory-bound
H100 ridge point (BF16 TC): 989 TFLOPS / 3.35 TB/s ≈ 295 FLOPs/byte
SAXPY (y=ax+y) at 1 FMA per 12 bytes = 0.083 FLOPs/byte — deeply memory-bound on all GPUs
SGEMM (C=A@B, N×N) at N/8 FLOPs/byte — compute-bound for N ≥ 295×8 ≈ 2360
commonMisconceptions: Achieving 100% of peak memory bandwidth is always possible — ECC, non-coalesced access, cache effects limit achievable BW
Memory bandwidth is independent of access pattern — strided/random access reduces effective bandwidth dramatically
Adding more computation to a memory-bound kernel slows it down — if memory-bound, compute is free up to the ridge point

exercises.title: Memory Bandwidth Benchmark Kernel
exercises.whatItTeaches: How to measure achieved memory bandwidth with CUDA events; vector copy kernel as bandwidth benchmark; achieving near-peak bandwidth with coalesced access.
exercises.starterCodeHint: cudaEvent_t start, stop; cudaEventRecord(start); vecCopy<<<grid,block>>>(dst,src,N); cudaEventRecord(stop); cudaEventSynchronize(stop); float ms; cudaEventElapsedTime(&ms, start, stop); float bw = 2.0f*N*sizeof(float)/ms/1e6;
exercises.testCaseHint: Should achieve >90% of peak HBM bandwidth with coalesced access; compare with nvbandwidthtest

coverageGaps: Constant memory now has an exercise (constant-memory-ray-tracer); texture memory still not covered — low priority for modern ML workloads
cudaMemcpy2D / pitched allocation for 2D arrays — useful for coalescing, not planned
Pinned (page-locked) memory for H2D transfer optimization — mentioned in best practices and CUDA by Example Ch10; no exercise yet
